Scenario.title: Ransomware Attack on Healthcare Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Healthcare and Public Health Complex A sophisticated ransomware campaign targeting legacy medical devices and PACS systems in healthcare organizations, leveraging AI-driven reconnaissance, supply chain compromises, and zero-day exploits to achieve persistent, stealthy lateral movement and data exfiltration.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Financial Gain through Ransom Payments and Data Extortion

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Target Artifact (Tasks), Script, OS Process, Dynamic Link Library, Network Traffic, Target System Artifact, User Account, Process, Network Traffic
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Script Execution (Mechanism), Ptrace System Calls, DLL Side-Loading, Network Traffic Content (Mechanism), Application Layer Protocol, Process Creation (Mechanism), Command Execution (Mechanism), Pass-the-Hash via HIPAA-Compliant File Shares, User Account Authentication (Mechanism), Replication Through Removable Media, Network Traffic Content (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attackers deploy AI algorithms to analyze publicly available medical device firmware versions and task schedules, identifying outdated systems with known vulnerabilities in PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication Systems) and IoT medical devices.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: Automated scripts are developed to harvest medical data formats (e.g., DICOM for imaging systems) and customize ransomware payloads tailored to exploit specific PACS and IoT device protocols.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Phishing emails are sent to third-party medical suppliers, embedding malicious links that exploit the Firefox/Thunderbird vulnerability to steal credentials and establish initial foothold in the supply chain.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC-640: Inclusion of Code in Existing Process
stage.description: Malicious code is injected into legacy medical device processes using ptrace system calls, exploiting the Chromium heap overflow to execute ransomware payloads on critical life-support equipment.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: A malicious DLL is placed in the WinSxS directory to persist across reboots, ensuring continuous access to diagnostic imaging systems and networked medical devices.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC-226: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: Hardcoded credentials embedded in network traffic are intercepted and leveraged to escalate privileges, granting full control over diagnostic imaging systems and PACS servers.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious traffic is disguised as legitimate medical telemetry data, evading detection by blending with normal application layer protocols used in hospital networks.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-670: Software Development Tools Maliciously Altered
stage.description: Compromised cloud instances host altered deployment tools that capture staff credentials during shift handoffs, feeding them into the ransomware command-and-control infrastructure.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Scripts are executed on network switches/routers to map unsegmented patient monitoring networks, identifying high-value targets for lateral movement.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Pass-the-hash attacks are conducted over HIPAA-compliant file shares to move laterally between segmented healthcare networks while avoiding detection.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-226: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: User account credentials are exploited to prioritize collection of patient records containing insurance and personal health information.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Data is exfiltrated via USB drives disguised as medical equipment components, mimicking legitimate maintenance procedures to avoid suspicion.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Network switches are remotely disabled to disrupt emergency response systems, forcing hospitals to pay ransoms to restore critical care operations.

Scenario.summary: This multi-stage ransomware campaign combines AI reconnaissance, supply chain phishing, and zero-day exploits to compromise healthcare IT infrastructure. By leveraging both technical vulnerabilities and human factors, the attackers achieve persistent access, lateral movement, and data exfiltration while evading detection through protocol obfuscation and physical sabotage.
